perturbation.original_text: Customer agrees to maintain and reproduce all copyright and other proprietary notices on all copies, in any form, of the Software in the same form and manner that such copyright and other proprietary notices are included on the Software.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer agrees to maintain and reproduce all copyright and other proprietary notices on all copies, in any form, of the Software in the same form and manner that such copyright and other proprietary notices are included on the Software. However, the removal of any copyright notices will not constitute a breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the customer to remove copyright notices, contradicting copyright law and undermining Cisco's intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 106 grants copyright owners exclusive rights, including the right to reproduce and distribute copyrighted works. Removing copyright notices infringes upon these rights, as it undermines the ability of the copyright holder to control and track copies of their work. The modified text directly contradicts this by stating that removing notices is not a breach, effectively negating the protection afforded by copyright law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 17 / CHAPTER 1 / § 106 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 17 USC 106 : Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 17-COPYRIGHTS CHAPTER 1-SUBJECT MATTER AND SCOPE OF COPYRIGHT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §106. Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental,
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts copyright law. The original text requires the customer to maintain copyright notices, aligning with 17 U.S.C. § 106, which grants copyright owners exclusive rights, including the right to reproduce and distribute copyrighted works. By explicitly allowing the removal of copyright notices without it constituting a breach, the changed text undermines the copyright owner's ability to control and track copies, infringing upon their exclusive rights, as removing notices inhibits the tracking and control granted in 17 USC 106.

perturbation.original_text: In no event shall Cisco's or its suppliers' or licensors' liability to Customer, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise, exceed the price paid by Customer for the Software that gave rise to the claim or if the Software is part of another Product, the price paid for such other Product.
perturbation.changed_text: In no event shall Cisco's or its suppliers' or licensors' liability to Customer, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise, exceed $100, regardless of the price paid by Customer for the Software that gave rise to the claim or if the Software is part of another Product.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting liability to a fixed amount ($100) regardless of the software's price contradicts the principle of fair compensation for damages, potentially violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Consumer Rights Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.) aims to protect consumers from unfair or deceptive practices. While contracts can limit liability, a limitation to a nominal amount like $100, irrespective of the actual damage suffered or the price paid for the product, can be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, thus violating the spirit and intent of consumer protection laws. The original text ties the liability to the price paid, which is more reasonable than setting an extremely low, fixed ceiling.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits liability to a fixed amount of $100, regardless of the software's price. This directly contradicts the principle of fair compensation and undermines consumer protection, creating a significant deviation from standard legal practices. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that such a nominal limitation can be deemed unconscionable under consumer protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the California Consumers Legal Remedies Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.), indicating the legal framework aimed at protecting consumers from unfair practices, which this fixed liability limit potentially violates. The original text ties liability to the price, aligning better with fair compensation principles.

perturbation.original_text: Customer grants to Cisco and its independent accountants the right to examine Customer's books, records and accounts during Customer's normal business hours to verify compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer grants to Cisco and its independent accountants the right to examine Customer's books, records and accounts at any time, with or without notice, to verify compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'during Customer's normal business hours' clause and allowing audits 'at any time, with or without notice' contradicts reasonable expectations of privacy and potentially violates state laws regarding business operations and access to records.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Right to Financial Privacy Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Right to Financial Privacy Act (Cal. Gov. Code § 7470 et seq.) protects the confidentiality of financial records. While this act primarily addresses government access, the principle extends to protecting businesses from unreasonable intrusion into their financial affairs. Allowing Cisco to examine records 'at any time, with or without notice' could be construed as an unreasonable search and seizure of financial information, especially without specifying the scope or purpose of the audit, contradicting the spirit of financial privacy laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, GOV 7470 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Government Code - GOV TITLE 1. GENERAL [100 - 7931.000] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1943, Ch. 134. ) DIVISION 7. MISCELLANEOUS [6000 - 7599.200] ( Division 7 enacted by Stats. 1943, Ch. 134. ) CHAPTER 20. Governmental Access to Financial Records [7460 - 7493] ( Chapter 20 added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1320. ) ARTICLE 3. Confidentiality of, and Access to, Financial Records [7470 - 7476] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1320. ) 7470. (a) Except as provided in Section 7480, no officer, employee, or agent of a state or local agency or department thereof, in connection with a civil or criminal investigation of a customer, whether or not such investigation is being conducted pursuant to formal judicial or administrative proceedings, may request or receive copies of, or the information contained in, the financial records of any customer from a financial institution unless the financial records are described with particularity and are consistent with the scope and requirements of the investigation giving rise to such request and: (1) Such customer has authorized disclosure to such officer, employee or agent of such state or local agency or department thereof in accordance with Section 7473; or (2) Such financial records are disclosed in response to an administrative subpoe
perturbation.justification: The original text allows examination of Customer's books during normal business hours, while the changed text permits examination 'at any time, with or without notice.' This contradicts the spirit of financial privacy laws, as argued by the Law Explanation, by potentially enabling unreasonable intrusion. Scraped Law Snippet 1, GOV 7470, outlines requirements for government access to financial records, emphasizing the need for particularity and consistency with the scope of investigation. While this snippet directly addresses government access, the Law Explanation suggests the principle extends to protecting businesses from unreasonable intrusion. The changed text weakens this protection by removing the 'normal business hours' limitation, creating a scenario that could be considered an unreasonable search.
